skill.name: webapp-sqlmap
skill.description: Guides authorized web application security testing with sqlmap for SQL injection detection, validation, database enumeration, and reporting.
skill.summary: Use sqlmap to validate SQL injection risk during authorized security assessments.
skill.author: AgentSecOps

security_audit.summary: The static findings are mixed: generic reference templates create many false positives, but the main skill contains confirmed dual-use offensive sqlmap guidance. The skill is not deceptive and includes authorization warnings, but it provides explicit workflows for data extraction, file access, OS shells, WAF evasion, and Tor use, so it should not be published without strict marketplace controls.

high_findings.title: Dual-use SQL injection exploitation workflow
high_findings.description: The main skill goes beyond detection and instructs users on database enumeration, table dumping, credential extraction, server file reads, file writes, and OS shell access. These are legitimate in authorized tests but can directly enable unauthorized compromise.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections explicitly describe data extraction, file access, and shell options in an sqlmap workflow. Authorization warnings reduce malicious-intent confidence but not operational risk.

high_findings.title: WAF bypass and anonymity guidance
high_findings.description: The skill teaches tamper scripts, random user agents, proxy use, Tor checks, method changes, and randomized delays. These techniques can support authorized testing, but they also lower barriers for evasion during unauthorized attacks.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited sections are semantically about bypassing blocking controls and changing request behavior. Legitimate security testing is possible, but evasion content is clearly present.

high_findings.title: Unsafe pipe-to-shell installer pattern in CI template
high_findings.description: The CI template includes a curl-to-shell installation pattern. If copied into a workflow, this executes remote code from the network during CI and creates supply-chain risk.
high_findings.confidence_reasoning: The pattern is a real remote-code execution risk in CI templates. It appears educational rather than malicious, but the dangerous usage is concrete.

medium_findings.title: Operational execution examples require strong scope controls
medium_findings.description: Many static external-command and network findings come from sqlmap command examples against example domains. The commands are not executed by the skill itself, but they are actionable instructions for invasive network testing.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The examples are documented commands rather than hidden code execution. Risk remains elevated because they are intended to be run by the user against web targets.

medium_findings.title: Credential and environment examples are mostly benign templates
medium_findings.description: The scanner flagged environment variables, token names, and secret examples in rule and CI templates. These are mostly demonstrative security-scanning examples, but they can normalize copying placeholder secrets or broad token access if used carelessly.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The semantic context is defensive examples and GitHub Actions configuration, so most secret hits are false positives. The confidence is medium because templates can still be copied into real workflows.

low_findings.title: Static script and crypto hits in references are false positives
low_findings.description: The document.write, innerHTML, MD5, SHA1, and API key patterns in the reference files are vulnerable-code examples used to teach detection and remediation. No evidence found that they are executable skill logic or hidden malicious behavior.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The surrounding text labels these snippets as examples, vulnerable patterns, and remediation material. This strongly supports a false-positive evaluation for those static hits.

low_findings.title: Prompt injection search found no evidence
low_findings.description: No evidence found in the analyzed files for override instructions, fake system messages, pre-approval claims, or instructions to skip security review.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: A targeted case-insensitive search across the listed files found no matching prompt-injection indicators. The confidence is high but limited to the inspected repository contents.

dangerous_patterns.title: sqlmap table and credential dumping
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes sqlmap workflows for dumping tables, selected credential columns, and all databases. This can expose sensitive data if used outside strict authorization.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited lines explicitly describe dump operations and credential-column extraction. This is direct evidence of data-extraction capability.

dangerous_patterns.title: sqlmap server file read and file write
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes guidance for reading server files and writing a PHP file to the web root through SQL injection. This can lead to sensitive file disclosure or web shell placement.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited section is labeled file system access and includes read and write operations. The web-root destination makes compromise impact clear.

dangerous_patterns.title: sqlmap OS shell and SQL shell options
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes guidance for operating-system command execution and interactive shells through sqlmap. This is a high-impact post-exploitation capability.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cited section is explicitly about OS command execution and shell access. Legitimate assessment use exists, but the capability is high risk.

dangerous_patterns.title: WAF bypass and Tor options
dangerous_patterns.description: The skill includes tamper scripts, random agents, proxying, and Tor options. These patterns can be used to evade controls and obscure request origin.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The section title and options are clear evidence of evasion guidance. It is not hidden, but it is risky dual-use content.

dangerous_patterns.title: Remote script execution in CI template
dangerous_patterns.description: The CI template downloads an installer over the network and pipes it directly to a shell. This is a known dangerous pattern for CI supply-chain exposure.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The line is in a template and not hidden payload code, but copying it into CI would execute unauthenticated remote script content.

content.value_statement: SQL injection testing can be risky and hard to document consistently. This skill structures authorized sqlmap workflows for detection, validation, enumeration, and remediation reporting.
actual_capabilities: Plans authorized SQL injection testing workflows with scope and data handling checks.
Explains sqlmap options for GET, POST, cookie, header, and request-file testing.
Guides database fingerprinting, database enumeration, table listing, and controlled data extraction.
Provides reporting guidance with OWASP, CWE, MITRE ATT&CK, PCI-DSS, and ISO references.
Includes operational safeguards for rate limiting, session handling, logging, and cleanup.
Adds defensive remediation guidance for parameterized queries, least privilege, and monitoring.
limitations: Requires explicit written authorization before testing any target system.
Does not replace human judgment for scope, legal approval, or production safety decisions.
Can produce invasive commands that may disrupt applications or expose sensitive data.
Does not verify that a target, credential, or request file is within the approved assessment scope.

use_cases.title: Authorized Penetration Test
use_cases.description: Structure a scoped sqlmap assessment, validate injection points, and document database exposure with remediation guidance.

use_cases.title: Developer Vulnerability Validation
use_cases.description: Reproduce a reported SQL injection issue in a controlled environment and confirm that a fix blocks exploitation.

use_cases.title: Audit Evidence Preparation
use_cases.description: Prepare concise evidence, impact statements, and control mappings for an authorized web application assessment.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Basic Check
prompt_templates.prompt: Help me plan an authorized sqlmap check for one in-scope URL. Include authorization, safe test settings, evidence to collect, and reporting notes.

prompt_templates.title: Review a Request File
prompt_templates.prompt: Review this captured HTTP request for in-scope SQL injection testing. Identify likely parameters, safe sqlmap options, and data handling precautions.

prompt_templates.title: Validate a Finding
prompt_templates.prompt: I have approval to validate a suspected SQL injection. Build a minimal sqlmap workflow that confirms impact without dumping unnecessary sensitive data.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare an Assessment Report
prompt_templates.prompt: Convert these authorized sqlmap results into a professional finding with impact, evidence summary, CWE and OWASP mapping, risk rating, and remediation steps.

output_examples.input: Plan a safe assessment for a staging login form with written authorization.
output_examples.output: The response starts with scope confirmation, selects low-impact detection settings, defines evidence boundaries, and lists remediation-focused report sections.

output_examples.input: Summarize controlled sqlmap results for a vulnerable search parameter.
output_examples.output: The response describes the affected parameter, confirmed injection type, database exposure level, business impact, and fixes without including sensitive dumped records.

output_examples.input: Create a remediation checklist after a SQL injection finding.
output: Use parameterized queries for all database calls.
Apply least privilege to application database accounts.
Add regression tests and monitoring for injection attempts.

best_practices: Confirm written authorization, scope, test window, and data handling rules before running sqlmap.
Start with low-impact detection and increase risk only when the engagement rules allow it.
Report summarized evidence and redact sensitive values from all deliverables.
anti_patterns: Testing public or third-party targets without explicit written permission.
Dumping full databases when a smaller proof of impact is sufficient.
Using WAF bypass, shell, or file-write options outside a clearly approved objective.

faq.question: Is this skill safe for production systems?
faq.answer: Use it only with written authorization and approved test windows. Some sqlmap options can disrupt services or expose sensitive data.

faq.question: Does it run sqlmap automatically?
faq.answer: No. It provides guidance and command planning. The user remains responsible for execution, scope, and approvals.

faq.question: Can it help write a remediation plan?
faq.answer: Yes. It includes defensive guidance such as parameterized queries, least privilege, safer error handling, and monitoring.

faq.question: Does it support authenticated testing?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill discusses cookies, headers, request files, and credentials for authorized authenticated testing.

faq.question: Why is the marketplace risk high?
faq.answer: The skill contains explicit exploitation workflows, data dumping, server file access, shell access, and WAF evasion guidance.

faq.question: Can Claude, Codex, and Claude Code use it?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill declares support for Claude, Codex, and Claude Code in the marketplace metadata.
